rationale: This example shows the classic Lewis acid-promoted rearrangement of a cyclic enol acetal derived from a 1,3-diol precursor (implied). The reaction converts the acyclic hydroxy enol ether into a substituted tetrahydropyran, which is the hallmark of the Petasis-Ferrier rearrangement. The substrates are simple and acyclic (apart from the necessary cyclic acetal portion), and the stereochemistry shown reflects the typical diastereoselective outcome of the reaction, forming a 2,4,6-trisubstituted tetrahydropyran common in polyketide synthesis applications.
collision_avoidance_note: This example is specific to the rearrangement of a cyclic enol acetal containing a pendant alcohol, which is rearranged to a substituted tetrahydropyran. It avoids the original Ferrier rearrangement (glycals), the Petasis olefination (titanocene methylenation), and generic Lewis acid-catalyzed acetal manipulations by featuring the characteristic structural transformation of this named variant. The use of a simple alkylaluminum Lewis acid is also characteristic.
